title: Leishmaniasis
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations

items.title: Transmission Modes of Leishmania Species
items.content: Leishmaniasis is transmitted by phlebotomine sandflies, with transmission being either anthroponotic or zoonotic. Human-to-human transmission can occur via shared infected needles among IV drug users.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs through phlebotomine sandflies: Phlebotomus in the Old World and Lutzomyia in the New World.
Transmission types include anthroponotic (human to human) and zoonotic (animal reservoir to humans).
Human-to-human transmission documented via shared infected needles among IV drug users.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.title: Life Cycle of Leishmania Organisms
items.content: The life cycle of Leishmania involves two forms: promastigotes in the sandfly vector and amastigotes in vertebrate hosts. Promastigotes are introduced into the host's skin, taken up by neutrophils, then macrophages where they transform into amastigotes.
keyPoints: Leishmania exists as extracellular promastigotes (10–20 μm) in sandflies and intracellular amastigotes (2–4 μm) in vertebrate hosts.
Promastigotes are introduced through the sandfly's proboscis into the host skin.
Neutrophils initially take up promastigotes, which may be released to macrophages upon apoptosis.
Amastigotes multiply inside macrophages, causing cell rupture and further invasion.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.title: Geographic Distribution and Incidence of Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: Visceral leishmaniasis (VL) is prevalent in specific regions such as East Africa, Brazil, and the Indian subcontinent. The incidence has significantly declined in some areas due to targeted elimination programs.
keyPoints: Leishmaniasis occurs in 99 countries—most of them developing—in tropical and temperate regions.
An estimated 30,000 new cases of VL occur annually.
In the Indian subcontinent, VL incidence has markedly declined with a reported 98.7% reduction in India.

items.title: Impact of HIV Co-infection on Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: HIV co-infection significantly impacts VL, particularly in Mediterranean Europe and parts of Africa, where it is associated with higher mortality due to compounded immunosuppression.
keyPoints: 70% of adult VL cases in Mediterranean Europe are associated with HIV co-infection.
~30% of VL patients in Ethiopia and 5% in India are co-infected with HIV.

items.title: Immunopathogenesis of Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: The immune response to VL involves a balance between proinflammatory cytokines and IL-10, which can suppress the immune system's ability to control the infection.
keyPoints: Successful immune responses involve IL-12 production by antigen-presenting cells and IFN-γ secretion by T helper 1 (TH1) lymphocytes.
Patients with active VL have elevated levels of IL-10, which can inhibit macrophage activation and promote parasite survival.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Progression of Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: Visceral leishmaniasis (VL) typically presents with an abrupt onset of moderate- to high-grade fever, rigor, and chills. The disease progresses with intermittent fevers over weeks, splenomegaly by the second week, followed by hepatomegaly. Weight loss, weakness, hyperpigmentation, hypoalbuminemia, severe anemia, thrombocytopenia-related bleeding, and secondary infections are common.
keyPoints: Affects all ages in India/Horn of Africa; immunocompetent infants/children and immunodeficient adults in Americas/Mediterranean.
Incubation period: 2–3 months to over a year.
Fever with rigor/chills, splenomegaly by second week, hepatomegaly follows.
Hypoalbuminemia may cause pedal edema and ascites; severe anemia can lead to congestive heart failure.
Thrombocytopenia associated with epistaxis, retinal hemorrhages, GI bleeding.
Secondary infections like pneumonia, tuberculosis common.
Untreated VL is fatal in most patients, 100% fatality in HIV co-infection.

items.title: Laboratory Diagnosis of Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: Diagnosis of VL is confirmed by detecting amastigotes in tissue aspirates, with splenic smears being the most sensitive. Serologic tests like ELISA and IFAT are used in sophisticated labs. The rK39 rapid diagnostic test (RDT) offers high sensitivity (~98%) and specificity (~90%) except in East Africa, where performance is lower.
keyPoints: Gold standard: detection of amastigotes in tissue aspirates.
Sensitivity: splenic smears >95%, bone marrow 60–85%, lymph node 50%.
rK39 RDT sensitivity ~98%, specificity ~90% (except East Africa).
PCR and real-time PCR offer high sensitivity and species identification but are limited to specialized labs.
LAMP of nucleic acids from blood samples is promising for point-of-care diagnostics.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis Considerations for Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: VL can be mistaken for malaria and other febrile illnesses like typhoid fever, tuberculosis, brucellosis, schistosomiasis, and histoplasmosis. Splenomegaly due to various causes may also mimic VL. Fever with neutropenia or pancytopenia in endemic regions suggests VL; hypergamaglobulinemia supports the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Mimics include malaria, typhoid fever, tuberculosis, brucellosis, schistosomiasis, histoplasmosis.
Splenomegaly from other causes (e.g., portal hypertension, chronic myeloid leukemia) may be confused with VL.
Fever with neutropenia/pancytopenia in endemic regions suggests VL.
Hyergamaglobulinemia in long-standing illness supports VL diagnosis.
In nonendemic areas, travel history is crucial for diagnosing VL.

items.title: Treatment Choice and Resistance in Leishmaniasis
items.content: Pentavalent antimonial compounds are the preferred treatment for visceral leishmaniasis (VL) in most endemic regions, but resistance is prevalent in Bihar, India. In such cases, amphotericin B or miltefosine should be used instead.
keyPoints: Pentavalent antimonials are first-line drugs globally except in Bihar due to resistance.
Amphotericin B (deoxycholate or liposomal) and miltefosine preferred in Bihar, India.
Liposomal AmB is the drug of choice in Mediterranean countries where cost is less of a concern.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.title: Dosage and Adverse Effects of Pentavalent Antimonials
items.content: Sodium stibogluconate and meglumine antimoniate are the two pentavalent antimonial preparations used, with a daily dose of 20 mg/kg. Treatment lasts for 28–30 days, but cure rates vary significantly by region due to resistance.
keyPoints: Sodium stibogluconate: 100 mg Sbᵅ/mL; meglumine antimoniate: 85 mg Sbᵅ/mL.
Daily dose is 20 mg/kg, administered via IV infusion or IM injection for 28–30 days.
Cure rates exceed 90% in Africa, Americas, and most of the Old World but are <50% in Bihar due to resistance.
Common adverse reactions include arthralgia, myalgia, elevated aminotransferases, and ECG changes; QTᵉ prolongation >0.5 s may indicate risk for ventricular arrhythmia.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.title: Liposomal Amphotericin B (LAmB) as Preferred Treatment for Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: LAmB is preferred over conventional AmB deoxycholate due to its better safety profile and ability to deliver high drug concentrations with minimal toxicity. It is extensively used globally for treating visceral leishmaniasis (VL).
keyPoints: LAmB has a terminal half-life of ~150 hours.
LAmB can be detected in liver and spleen for weeks after administration.
FDA-approved regimen: 3 mg/kg daily on days 1–5, 14, and 21 (total dose, 21 mg/kg).
Total dose varies by region: Asia (10–15 mg/kg), Africa (~18 mg/kg), Mediterranean/American regions (≥20 mg/kg).
Single-dose regimen of 10 mg/kg cured infection in 96% of patients in India.
Adverse effects are usually mild, including infusion reactions, backache, and occasional reversible nephrotoxicity.

items.title: Managing Adverse Effects of Conventional Amphotericin B (AmB)
items.content: Conventional AmB deoxycholate is associated with significant adverse effects, including fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, and thrombophlebitis. Pre-infusion administration of antihistamines and antipyretics can minimize acute toxicities.
keyPoints: Doses: 0.75–1.0 mg/kg on alternate days for a total of 15 infusions.
Common adverse reactions: fever with chills, nausea, vomiting, thrombophlebitis.
Acute toxicities minimized by chlorpheniramine and acetaminophen before infusion.
Potential severe toxicities: renal dysfunction, hypokalemia, hypersensitivity reactions, bone marrow suppression, myocarditis (rare but fatal).

items.title: Paromomycin: Effective and Safe Treatment for VL with Specific Dosing
items.content: Paromomycin is an aminocyclitol-aminoglycoside antibiotic used in India to treat visceral leishmaniasis (VL) with high efficacy. It has a cure rate of 94.6% when administered intramuscularly at 11 mg/kg daily for 21 days, though its mechanism against Leishmania is not fully established.
keyPoints: Cure rate: 94.6% in VL treatment
Dose: 11 mg/kg IM daily for 21 days
Adverse effects: Hepatotoxicity, reversible ototoxicity, rare nephrotoxicity and tetany

items.title: Miltefosine: Oral Treatment for Leishmaniasis with Considerations for Resistance and Adverse Effects
items.content: Miltefosine is the first oral treatment approved in several countries, including the U.S., for leishmaniasis. It has a long half-life (150–200 hours) and requires careful dosing to avoid resistance. The cure rate is ~94% in India, but recent studies show declining effectiveness.
keyPoints: Cure rate: ~94% in India
Dosing: 28 days; <25 kg: 50 mg twice daily, ≥25 kg: 50 mg twice daily, children 2–11 years: 2.5 mg/kg
Adverse effects: Vomiting (40%), diarrhea (20%), rare severe allergic dermatitis, hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity
Resistance risk due to long half-life
Contraindicated in pregnancy and for women of childbearing age without contraception for 5 months post-treatment

items.title: Advantages of Multidrug Therapy for Visceral Leishmaniasis
items.content: Multidrug therapy is preferred due to better compliance, lower costs, reduced toxicity, and decreased likelihood of resistance. In India, combinations such as LAmB followed by miltefosine or paromomycin achieved >97% cure rates.
keyPoints: Potential advantages include better compliance, lower costs, less toxicity, and reduced resistance.
>97% cure rate with specific drug combinations in India.
17-day regimen of SbV and paromosin is preferred in Africa.

items.title: Treatment Efficacy and Regimen Comparisons
items.content: A phase 3 trial compared the standard 17-day regimen with a 14-day combination of paromycin and miltefosine, showing similar cure rates. The new combination is proposed as an alternative.
keyPoints: Phase 3 trial in East Africa showed similar efficacy between 17-day and 14-day regimens.
New combination of paromycin and miltefosine for 14 days is a viable alternative.

items.title: Management of HIV/VL Co-infection
items.content: HIV co-infection complicates VL treatment, often presenting atypically. LAmB is preferred for primary and relapse treatment. WHO now recommends LAmB with miltefosine in the Indian subcontinent and East Africa.
keyPoints: HIV increases risk of VL by several-fold in endemic areas.
LAmB (40 mg/kg) is optimal for Americas and Mediterranean; 30 mg/kg for India/East Africa with miltefosine.
Antiretroviral therapy reduces relapse frequency, essential for management.

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment Challenges in PKDL
items.content: In Post-Kala-azar Dermal Leishmaniasis (PKDL), parasites are more easily detected in nodular lesions than hypopigmented macules due to heavier cellular infiltrates. Diagnosis relies on clinical findings, slit skin smears, or PCR for parasitic DNA, as serology remains positive post-treatment of visceral leishmaniasis (VL). Treatment challenges include toxicity and noncompliance with prolonged antimonial therapy, high costs of AmB regimens, and declining efficacy of miltefosine.
keyPoints: Parasites more easily detected in nodular lesions than macules.
Diagnosis: history, clinical findings, slit skin smears, PCR for parasitic DNA.
Serology remains positive post-VL cure; low sensitivity of skin smears.
Prolonged antimonial therapy (up to 120 days) is toxic and leads to noncompliance.
Alternative AmB regimen: three-four 20-day courses over months, expensive.
Oral miltefosine for 12 weeks shows declining efficacy; ocular toxicity in 3.7% of patients.
LAmB alone or with miltefosine (3 weeks) >80% efficacy in PKDL treatment.

items.title: Regional Variations and Treatment Efficacy in PKDL
items.content: In the Indian subcontinent, oral miltefosine is recommended for PKDL despite declining efficacy and ocular toxicity. LAmB alone or combined with miltefosine shows over 80% efficacy. In East Africa, spontaneous healing occurs in many patients; those with persistent lesions respond well to 60 days of pentavalent antimonial treatment.
keyPoints: Indian subcontinent: oral miltefosine for 12 weeks recommended despite declining efficacy and 3.7% ocular toxicity.
>80% efficacy with LAmB alone or combined with miltefosine (3 weeks).
East Africa: majority experience spontaneous healing; good response to 60 days of pentavalent antimonial in persistent cases.

items.title: Epidemiology and Transmission of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (CL)
items.content: Cutaneous leishmaniasis is divided into Old World and New World forms. Old World CL, caused by Leishmania tropica, is anthroponotic and found in urban/suburban areas. Zoonotic CL, often due to L. major, involves desert rodents as reservoirs across the Middle East, Africa, and central Asia. Major outbreaks are linked with population movement in regions like Afghanistan and Syria. New World CL is mainly zoonotic, involving forest animals as reservoirs, with L. braziliensis adapting to urban settings.
keyPoints: Old World CL caused by Leishmania tropica is anthroponotic and urban/suburban.
Zoonotic CL often due to Leishmania major involves desert rodents in the Middle East, Africa, central Asia.
Major outbreaks linked with population movement in Afghanistan, Syria, Iraq, Lebanon, Turkey.
New World CL mainly zoonotic, involving forest animals; L. braziliensis adapting to urban settings.

items.title: Clinical Features and Healing of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis
items.content: After a sandfly bite, CL manifests as a papule that ulcerates over weeks or months. Lesions are typically painless with necrotic tissue at the base. They may be single or multiple, ranging from 0.5 to >3 cm in size. Healing occurs spontaneously within 2–15 months, with L. major and L. mexicana lesions healing rapidly, while L. tropica and Viannia subspecies heal more slowly. Recidivans can occur with L. tropica, and L. mexicana may cause destructive lesions on the ear.
keyPoints: Papule develops post-sandfly bite, ulcerates over weeks/months.
Lesions typically painless, necrotic tissue at base, 0.5 to >3 cm in size.
Healing spontaneous within 2–15 months; L. major/L. mexicana heal rapidly.
L. tropica and Viannia subspecies heal slowly; recidivans possible with L. tropica.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Laboratory Confirmation of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis
items.content: A history of an insect bite followed by ulceration in endemic areas suggests CL. Differential diagnoses include cutaneous tuberculosis, fungal infections, leprosy, sarcoidosis, and malignant ulcers. Laboratory diagnosis involves detecting amastigotes via microscopy or PCR from lesion samples, with PCR offering species-level identification and treatment monitoring.
keyPoints: History of insect bite followed by ulceration in endemic areas suggests CL.
Differential diagnoses: cutaneous tuberculosis, fungal infections, leprosy, sarcoidosis, malignant ulcers.
Laboratory diagnosis via detection of amastigotes; PCR allows species-level identification and treatment monitoring.

items.title: Indications for Systemic Treatment of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis
items.content: Systemic treatment is necessary for cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL) in cases involving lesions over the face, hands, or joints; multiple lesions; large ulcers; lymphatic spread; New World CL with potential for mucosal involvement; and CL in HIV-co-infected patients. Topical agents may suffice for small lesions from self-healing species.
keyPoints: Systemic treatment required for specific lesion locations (face, hands, joints) or conditions (multiple lesions, large ulcers, lymphatic spread).
New World CL with potential for mucosal involvement and HIV-co-infected patients require systemic therapy.
Topical agents can be used for small lesions from self-healing species.

items.title: First-Line Treatment and Alternatives for Cutaneous Leishmaniasis
items.content: Pentavalent antimonials are the first-line treatment for all forms of CL, administered at 20 mg/kg for 20 days. Exceptions include L. guyanensis (treated with pentamidine isethionate) and L. aethiopica (responds to paromomycin but not antimonials). Miltefosine is increasingly preferred due to its efficacy.
keyPoints: Pentavalent antimonial: 20 mg/kg for 20 days, first-line treatment for CL.
L. guyanensis: treated with pentamidine isethionate (4 mg salt/kg, two injections 48 hours apart).
L. aethiopica: responds to paromomycin (16 mg/kg daily), not antimonials.
Miltefosine: oral dose of 2.5 mg/kg for 28 days, preferred in New World CL.

items.title: Management and Challenges of Diffuse Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (DCL)
items.content: Diffuse cutaneous leishmaniasis (DCL) is a rare form caused by L. amazonensis, L. mexicana, or L. aethiopica, characterized by uncontrolled parasite multiplication due to lack of cell-mediated immune response. Treatment includes repeated 20-day courses of pentavalent antimonials with drug-free intervals and combinations like paromomycin with sodium stibogluconate.
keyPoints: DCL caused by L. amazonensis, L. mexicana (South/Central America), or L. aethiopica (Ethiopia/Kenya).
Characterized by lack of cell-mediated immune response and high levels of immunosuppressive cytokines.
Treatment: repeated 20-day courses of pentavalent antimonials with drug-free intervals; combinations like paromomycin (14 mg/kg/day) and sodium stibogluconate (10 mg/kg/day).

items.title: Etiology and Risk Factors of Mucosal Leishmaniasis (ML)
items.content: Mucosal leishmaniasis is primarily caused by L. (V.) braziliensis, with rare cases attributed to other species such as L. amazonensis, L. (V.) guyanensis, and L. (V.) panamensis. Young men with chronic cutaneous leishmaniasis are at a higher risk of developing ML. Approximately 3% of those infected will develop mucosal involvement.
keyPoints: ML is typically caused by L. (V.) braziliensis, rarely by other species like L. amazonensis, L. (V.) guyanensis, and L. (V.) panamensis.
~3% of infected persons develop ML.
Young men with chronic lesions of cutaneous leishmaniasis are at particular risk.
ML is almost entirely confined to the Americas.

items.title: Clinical Features and Pathogenesis of Mucosal Leishmaniasis (ML)
items.content: The immune response in ML is characterized by a TH1 polarization, with increased levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α. Patients exhibit a stronger delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) response compared to cutaneous leishmaniasis. The disease typically presents as nasal symptoms progressing to severe tissue destruction. Despite strong immunity, ML does not heal spontaneously.
keyPoints: Immune response in ML is polarized toward a TH1 response with increased IFN-γ and TNF-α.
Patients have a stronger DTH response with ML than with CL.
Typical presentation includes nasal stuffiness, bleeding, destruction of nasal cartilage, and potential collapse of the nasal bridge.
ML does not heal spontaneously despite high TH1 immunity.

items.title: First-Line Treatment for Mucosal Leishmaniasis
items.content: Pentavalent antimonial agents are the first-line treatment for mucosal leishmaniasis, administered at a dose of 20 mg Sb^V/kg for 30 days. If therapy fails or relapses occur, and patients become unresponsive due to presumed resistance, AmB (amphotericin B) should be used as an alternative.
keyPoints: First-line treatment: Pentavalent antimonial agent
Dose: 20 mg Sb^V/kg for 30 days
Alternative if resistant: AmB deoxycholate, 25–45 mg/kg total dose

items.title: Treatment Alternatives and Prognosis in Mucosal Leishmaniasis
items.content: For patients unresponsive to antimonial therapy, AmB deoxycholate (25–45 mg/kg) or LAmB (2–3 mg/kg for 20 days) can be used. Miltefosine has shown efficacy with a cure rate of 71% in ML patients in Bolivia. The prognosis worsens with more extensive disease; hence, prompt and effective treatment along with regular follow-up is crucial.
keyPoints: AmB deoxycholate dose: 25–45 mg/kg
LAmB dose: 2–3 mg/kg for 20 days
Miltefosine cure rate in Bolivia: 71%
Prognosis worsens with more extensive disease

items.question: Which vector transmits Leishmania species in the 'New World'?
items.explanation: In the New World, Leishmania is transmitted by sandflies of the genus Lutzomyia.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.question: What form do Leishmania organisms take inside vertebrate hosts?
items.explanation: Inside vertebrate hosts, Leishmania organisms exist as nonflagellate amastigotes.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.question: How do promastigotes enter the vertebrate host?
options: Through the proboscis of a female sandfly
By direct contact with infected skin
items.explanation: Promastigotes are introduced into the vertebrate host through the proboscis of the female sandfly.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.question: Which species of Leishmania are responsible for anthroponotic and zoonotic transmission, respectively?
items.explanation: L. donovani is responsible for anthroponotic transmission, while L. infantum is responsible for zoonotic transmission.

items.question: In which region has VL been recently eliminated as a public health problem according to WHO?
items.explanation: The World Health Organization announced the elimination of VL as a public health problem in Bangladesh.

items.question: What percentage decline in VL incidence was reported by India?
items.explanation: India reported a 98.7% decline in the incidence of visceral leishmaniasis.

items.question: What is the primary reservoir for Mediterranean VL caused by L. infantum?
items.explanation: Mediterranean visceral leishmaniasis has a large canine reservoir.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most commonly associated with visceral leishmaniasis on the Indian subcontinent?
items.explanation: Hepatomegaly usually follows fever and is a common symptom in VL.

items.question: What is the sensitivity of splenic smears for diagnosing visceral leishmaniasis?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of splenic smears in diagnosing VL is greater than 95%.

items.question: Which test is used worldwide for rapid diagnosis of visceral leishmaniasis based on antibody detection?
items.explanation: The rK39 rapid diagnostic test (RDT) is used worldwide for quick antibody-based diagnosis.

items.question: In which region does the rK39 RDT have lower sensitivity and specificity?
items.explanation: The rK39 RDT has lower sensitivity and specificity in East Africa.

items.question: Which drug is preferred for the treatment of visceral leishmaniasis (VL) in Bihar, India due to antimony resistance?
items.explanation: In Bihar, India, there is widespread resistance to antimony; hence, either amphotericin B (deoxycholate or liposomal) or miltefosine is preferred.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.question: What is the cure rate for pentavalent antimonial treatment in Bihar, India?
items.explanation: Cure rates for pentavalent antimonial treatment are <50% in Bihar, India due to resistance.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.question: What is a common adverse reaction to Sbᵅ treatment that may require monitoring but not necessarily discontinuation?
items.explanation: Chemical pancreatitis is common with Sbᵅ treatment but usually does not require discontinuation.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.question: What is the preferred formulation of Amphotericin B in regions outside the Indian Subcontinent due to its better safety profile?
items.explanation: Liposomal AmB (LAmB) is preferred due to its better safety profile.

items.question: Which adverse reaction is almost universal with conventional AmB deoxycholate infusions?
items.explanation: Fever with chills is an almost universal adverse reaction to AmB infusions.

items.question: What is the FDA-approved regimen for LAmB in treating visceral leishmaniasis (VL) in terms of dosage and schedule?
options: 0.75–1.0 mg/kg on alternate days for a total of 15 infusions
items.explanation: The FDA-approved regimen is 3 mg/kg daily on days 1–5, 14, and 21.

items.question: Which drug is approved for the treatment of visceral leishmaniasis (VL) in India at an intramuscular dose of 11 mg/kg daily for 21 days?
items.explanation: Paromomycin is approved in India for VL treatment at this specific dose.

items.question: What is a common adverse effect of miltefosine that usually subsides after a few days?
items.explanation: Mild to moderate vomiting is a common adverse effect of miltefosine that usually subsides after a few days.

items.question: What is the recommended duration for miltefosine treatment in leishmaniasis?
items.explanation: The recommended therapeutic regimen consists of 28 consecutive days of miltefosine treatment.

items.question: What is the sensitivity level of skin smears in diagnosing PKDL?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of skin smears for diagnosing PKDL is quite low.

items.question: Which cells are the dominant cellular infiltrates in nodular lesions of PKDL?
items.explanation: In nodular lesions of PKDL, lymphocytes are the dominant cells.

items.question: What is a reported side effect of oral miltefosine treatment in PKDL?
items.explanation: There are reports of ocular toxicity with oral miltefosine treatment in PKDL.

items.question: Which Leishmania species is primarily responsible for Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis in urban areas?
items.explanation: Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis in urban areas is primarily caused by Leishmania tropica, which is anthroponotic.

items.question: Which of the following statements about New World cutaneous leishmaniasis is correct?
options: Leishmania tropica is a common cause.
Urbanization has led to increased cases due to Leishmania (Viannia) braziliensis.
Most infections are seen in urban areas.
items.explanation: New World cutaneous leishmaniasis is mainly zoonotic, and urbanization has led to increased cases of Leishmania (Viannia) braziliensis.

items.question: What is the typical clinical progression after a sandfly bite in cutaneous leishmaniasis?
options: Immediate ulceration without prior papule formation.
Papule develops, grows into a nodule, and then ulcerates over weeks or months.
Rapid healing of lesions within days.
Lesions do not ulcerate but remain as nodules.
items.explanation: Typically, after a sandfly bite in cutaneous leishmaniasis, a papule develops and grows into a nodule that ulcerates over weeks or months.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is considered the gold standard for confirming cutaneous leishmaniasis?
options: Culture of smear or biopsy material
Microscopic examination of slit skin smears, aspirates, or biopsies
items.explanation: The gold standard for confirming cutaneous leishmaniasis is the demonstration of amastigotes in material obtained from a lesion.

items.question: Which drug is the first-line treatment for all forms of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis?
items.explanation: A pentavalent antimonial is the first-line drug for all forms of CL.

items.question: Which species of Leishmania does not respond to antimonials and requires paromomycin as treatment?
items.explanation: CL due to L. aethiopica responds to paromomycin but not to antimonials.

items.question: For which species of Leishmania is pentamidine isethionate the drug of choice?
items.explanation: Pentamidine isethionate is the drug of choice for CL caused by Leishmania (Viannia) guyanensis.

items.question: Which treatment has shown to cure Cutaneous Leishmaniasis more rapidly than antimonials alone in Peru?
items.explanation: In Peru, topical imiquimod (5–7.5%) plus parenteral antimonials have been shown to cure CL more rapidly than antimonials alone.

items.question: Which species is most commonly responsible for mucosal leishmaniasis (ML)?
items.explanation: Mucosal leishmaniasis is typically caused by L. (V.) braziliensis.

items.question: What percentage of infected persons develop mucosal leishmaniasis?
items.explanation: Overall, approximately 3% of infected individuals develop mucosal leishmaniasis.

items.question: Which immune response is predominantly observed in patients with mucosal leishmaniasis?
items.explanation: The immune response in mucosal leishmaniasis is polarized toward a $ T_{H}1 $ response.

items.question: What is the first-line regimen of choice for treating mucosal leishmaniasis?
options: A pentavalent antimonial agent administered at a dose of 20 mg of Sb^V/kg for 30 days
LAmB administration of 2–3 mg/kg for 20 days
items.explanation: The regimen of choice is a pentavalent antimonial agent administered at the specified dose.

items.question: What should be used when patients with mucosal leishmaniasis become unresponsive to antimonial therapy?
options: Another course of a pentavalent antimonial agent
LAmB administration of 2–3 mg/kg for 20 days
items.explanation: AmB should be used when patients become unresponsive to antimonial therapy.

items.question: What is the cure rate of miltefosine in ML patients in Bolivia?
items.explanation: Miltefosine cured 71% of ML patients in Bolivia.

items.statement: Human-to-human transmission of Leishmania can occur via shared infected needles.
items.explanation: Human-to-human transmission has been documented in IV drug users through shared infected needles.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.statement: In utero transmission of Leishmania is a common occurrence.
items.explanation: In utero transmission to the fetus occurs rarely.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · Leishmaniasis | ETIOLOGY AND LIFE CYCLE

items.statement: VL is common in West and sub-Saharan Africa.
items.explanation: VL is rare in West and sub-Saharan Africa.

items.statement: IL-10 enhances the immune response against VL by promoting parasitic killing.
items.explanation: IL-10 suppresses the immune response and conditions macrophages for enhanced survival of the parasite.

items.statement: Leukopenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia occur sequentially in visceral leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: Leukopenia and anemia occur early, followed by thrombocytopenia.

items.statement: Renal dysfunction is a common complication of visceral leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: Renal dysfunction is uncommon in visceral leishmaniasis.

items.statement: Pentavalent antimonial compounds are the drug of choice in most endemic regions worldwide.
items.explanation: Pentavalent antimonials are indeed the drug of choice in many endemic regions, except where resistance is prevalent.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.statement: The daily dose for pentavalent antimonial treatment is 10 mg/kg by IV infusion or IM injection.
items.explanation: The correct daily dose for pentavalent antimonial treatment is 20 mg/kg.
items.reference: Leishmaniasis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | PENTAVALENT ANTIMONIAL COMPOUNDS

items.statement: LAmB can be detected in the liver and spleen for several weeks after a single dose.
items.explanation: With a terminal half-life of ~150 h, LAmB is detectable in tissues for weeks.

items.statement: Acute toxicities from AmB deoxycholate can be completely eliminated with antihistamines and antipyretic agents.
items.explanation: Antihistamines and antipyretics minimize, but do not eliminate, acute toxicities.

items.statement: Miltefosine has a short half-life, making it less prone to induce resistance in Leishmania.
items.explanation: Miltefosine has a long half-life (150–200 h), which makes it prone to inducing resistance.

items.statement: Paromomycin is used in combination with Sb V for the treatment of leishmaniasis in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.explanation: Paromomycin, combined with Sb V, is indeed used in sub-Saharan Africa.

items.statement: Miltefosine and paromomycin can be used simultaneously for 10 days as part of a multidrug therapy regimen.
items.explanation: The passage states that miltefosine and paromomycin were used together for 10 days.

items.statement: HIV infection does not affect the risk of developing VL in endemic areas.
items.explanation: HIV infection increases the risk of VL development by several-fold.

items.statement: Serology remains positive for several years after the cure of visceral leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: Positive serology does not help as it remains positive for several years after the cure of VL.

items.statement: The efficacy of LAmB alone or in combination with miltefosine in PKDL is less than 80%.
items.explanation: The efficacy was greater than 80% with both regimens.

items.statement: Leishmania tropica infections often result in lesions that heal rapidly.
items.explanation: Lesions due to Leishmania tropica tend to heal more slowly compared to those caused by L. major and L. mexicana.

items.statement: PCR or LAMP can identify Leishmania species to the level of subspecies.
items.explanation: PCR or LAMP is more sensitive than microscopy and culture, allowing identification of Leishmania to the species level.

items.statement: Diffuse Cutaneous Leishmaniasis (DCL) lesions typically ulcerate.
items.explanation: In DCL, the lesions do not ulcerate.

items.statement: Miltefosine is recommended for New World CL caused by L. panamensis and L. mexicana.
items.explanation: There is a strong recommendation for oral miltefosine treatment for New World CL where L. panamensis, L. mexicana, L. guyanensis, and L. braziliensis are the species involved.

items.statement: Every patient with mucosal leishmaniasis has a history of prior cutaneous leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: Not every patient with mucosal leishmaniasis has a history of prior cutaneous leishmaniasis.

items.statement: The strongly positive DTH response can distinguish between past and present infection in mucosal leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: The strongly positive DTH response fails to distinguish between past and present infections.

items.statement: Controlled trials have been conducted for LAmB in the treatment of mucosal leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: There are no controlled trials of LAmB.

items.statement: Prompt, effective treatment and regular follow-up improve the prognosis for mucosal leishmaniasis.
items.explanation: The more extensive the disease, the worse is the prognosis; thus, prompt, effective treatment and regular follow-up are essential.

items.assertion: The majority of individuals infected by L. donovani or L. infantum develop symptomatic disease.
items.reason: These individuals do not mount a successful immune response and control the infection.
items.explanation: Most individuals infected with L. donovani or L. infantum mount a successful immune response and never develop symptomatic disease.

items.assertion: IL-10 blockade can enhance antigen-specific IFN-γ responses in VL patients.
items.reason: IL-10 promotes parasitic killing by enhancing the immune response.
items.explanation: While IL-10 blockade does enhance antigen-specific IFN-γ responses, IL-10 actually inhibits parasitic killing.

items.assertion: The rK39 rapid diagnostic test cannot be used to measure cure or detect relapse of visceral leishmaniasis.
items.reason: These antibody detection tests remain positive for years after cure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rK39 cannot be used to measure cure or detect relapse.

items.assertion: Lymphadenopathy is a common symptom of visceral leishmaniasis in all endemic regions.
items.reason: Lymphadenopathy is rare in the Indian subcontinent but common elsewhere.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because lymphadenopathy is not common in the Indian subcontinent.

items.assertion: LAmB is used extensively to treat VL worldwide.
items.reason: Lipid formulations of AmB are preferentially taken up by reticuloendothelial tissues, reducing toxicity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why LAmB is used extensively.

items.assertion: Restoration of CD4+ T-cell count to >200/μL decreases the frequency of relapse in HIV-VL co-infection.
items.reason: Antiretroviral therapy helps restore immunity, reducing relapses.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and related; restoring CD4+ count reduces relapse due to improved immunity.

items.assertion: LAmB is the drug of choice for HIV/VL co-infection in all regions.
items.reason: It has been approved by the FDA and WHO for treatment in various regions.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but LAmB is not universally preferred; it varies by region.

items.assertion: Prolonged courses of pentavalent antimonials are frequently associated with noncompliance in treating Indian PKDL.
items.reason: These prolonged courses were toxic and led to noncompliance.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains it.

items.assertion: Leishmaniasis recidivans occurs in cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica.
items.reason: New lesions develop in the center or periphery of a healed sore due to L. tropica infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the occurrence of leishmaniasis recidivans.

items.assertion: Cutaneous tuberculosis can be mistaken for cutaneous leishmaniasis.
items.reason: Both conditions present with similar ulcerative skin lesions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, as cutaneous tuberculosis is sometimes mistaken for CL due to similar lesion presentations.

items.assertion: Miltefosine is a major advancement in the treatment of Cutaneous Leishmaniasis.
items.reason: Orally administered miltefosine makes it very attractive and has been recommended for New World CL.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: DCL lesions heal spontaneously over time.
items.reason: DCL is characterized by uncontrolled multiplication of parasites due to lack of a cell-mediated immune response.
items.explanation: A is false because DCL does not heal spontaneously. R is true as it describes the immunological characteristic of DCL.

items.assertion: Mucosal leishmaniasis does not heal spontaneously despite a strong $ T_{H}1 $ immunity.
items.reason: The immune response in mucosal leishmaniasis is polarized toward a $ T_{H}1 $ response with marked increases of IFN- $ \\$gamma $ and TNF-$ \alpha $.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why mucosal leishmaniasis does not heal spontaneously.
